source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Stationary sequence
summary: A sequence X_0, X_1, ... is stationary when each shifted finite block has the same law as the original block.
proof_idea: Check equality of all finite-dimensional distributions after shifting by k.
exam_use: Use as the entry point for ergodic averages, stationary Markov chains, rotations, and stationary increments.
pitfalls: Stationarity is distributional, not pathwise equality; it does not imply independence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Measure-preserving transformation model
summary: Every stationary sequence on a nice state space can be represented as X_n(omega)=X(phi^n omega) for a measure-preserving map phi.
proof_idea: Put the stationary law on sequence space and let phi be the left shift; stationarity makes the shift preserve probability.
exam_use: Lets all ergodic-theorem statements be proved for one abstract dynamical system.
pitfalls: The representation changes the sample space; it preserves laws but not necessarily the user's original probability space.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Invariant events and invariant sigma-field
summary: An event A is invariant if phi^{-1}A=A up to null sets; the invariant events form the sigma-field I.
proof_idea: Closure under complements and countable unions follows from pulling sets back through phi^{-1}.
exam_use: The limit in Birkhoff's theorem is the conditional expectation onto I.
pitfalls: Invariant means unchanged under the transformation, not merely high probability overlap after one shift.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Ergodicity
summary: A measure-preserving transformation is ergodic when every invariant event has probability 0 or 1.
proof_idea: Interpret nonergodicity as the ability to split the space into invariant pieces of positive probability.
exam_use: Use ergodicity to turn Birkhoff limits E(X|I) into constants EX.
pitfalls: Ergodic is stronger than stationary and different from independent; periodic examples can be stationary but not mixing.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: IID shifts are ergodic
summary: For an iid sequence, invariant shift events lie in the tail sigma-field, so Kolmogorov's 0-1 law implies ergodicity.
proof_idea: Iterate shift invariance to show A is measurable with respect to sigma(X_n, X_{n+1}, ...), then intersect over n.
exam_use: Explains why Birkhoff's theorem recovers the strong law for iid sequences.
pitfalls: The tail sigma-field argument uses iid independence; it is not automatic for every stationary sequence.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Stationary Markov chain ergodicity criterion
summary: A countable-state Markov chain started in a positive stationary distribution is ergodic exactly when the chain is irreducible.
proof_idea: Closed irreducible classes give invariant events; conversely the Markov property and recurrence force invariant probabilities to be constant.
exam_use: Use for time averages of irreducible positive recurrent chains.
pitfalls: Irreducibility makes I trivial, but periodic chains may still have a nontrivial tail sigma-field.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Circle rotation ergodicity
summary: Rotation x -> x+theta mod 1 is ergodic under Lebesgue measure when theta is irrational and not ergodic when theta is rational.
proof_idea: For irrational theta, Fourier coefficients of an invariant L2 function satisfy c_k(e^{2 pi i k theta}-1)=0, forcing all nonconstant coefficients to vanish.
exam_use: The canonical deterministic example where ergodic averages behave like probabilistic averages.
pitfalls: Rational rotations have finite orbits and many invariant unions of orbit slices.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Functions of stationary processes
summary: Measurable functions of the future of a stationary process form another stationary process; ergodicity is inherited under such factors.
proof_idea: Write Y_k=g(X_k,X_{k+1},...) and compare shifted finite-dimensional distributions.
exam_use: Useful for creating stationary indicator, block, or return-time processes.
pitfalls: The function must be applied in a shift-compatible way; arbitrary time-dependent functions can destroy stationarity.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Birkhoff ergodic theorem
summary: For X in L1, the time averages n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X(phi^m omega) converge almost surely and in L1 to E(X|I).
proof_idea: Subtract E(X|I), use the maximal ergodic lemma to rule out positive limsup and negative liminf, then truncate for L1 convergence.
exam_use: Main tool for replacing long-run empirical averages by conditional or ordinary means.
pitfalls: Without ergodicity the limit is random: it is E(X|I), not necessarily EX.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Maximal ergodic lemma
summary: For partial sums S_k of X along a measure-preserving orbit and M_k=max(0,S_1,...,S_k), one has E(X; M_k>0) >= 0.
proof_idea: Compare X(omega)+M_k(phi omega) with each S_j(omega), integrate, and use measure preservation.
exam_use: The key technical inequality behind Birkhoff's pointwise convergence proof.
pitfalls: It is an integration inequality, not a direct maximal probability bound; track the set {M_k>0}.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Strong law as an ergodic theorem
summary: For iid X_m in L1, Birkhoff gives n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X_m -> E X_0 almost surely and in L1.
proof_idea: The iid shift is ergodic, so E(X_0|I)=E X_0.
exam_use: Use as a conceptual shortcut from Chapter 2 LLNs to general stationary sequences.
pitfalls: The theorem needs integrability; for nonintegrable iid variables this conclusion can fail.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Stationary Markov chain time averages
summary: For an irreducible countable-state Markov chain started from its stationary distribution pi, n^{-1} sum_{m<n} f(X_m) -> sum_x f(x) pi(x) for pi-integrable f.
proof_idea: Apply Birkhoff to f(X_0) and use the irreducible stationary chain's ergodicity.
exam_use: Recovers the long-run frequency and reward law for positive recurrent Markov chains.
pitfalls: The statement is under stationarity; nonstationary initial laws need an additional argument.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Weyl equidistribution from irrational rotation
summary: For irrational theta, the orbit x+n theta mod 1 visits every interval [a,b) with limiting frequency b-a for every starting x.
proof_idea: Apply Birkhoff to interval indicators, then remove the exceptional set by approximating intervals from inside and using density.
exam_use: Use for deterministic averaging and number-theoretic distribution mod 1.
pitfalls: Birkhoff first gives almost-every starting point; the interval theorem needs extra work for every x.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Benford law for powers
summary: If log_10 2 is irrational, the first digit of 2^m has limiting frequency log_10((k+1)/k) for digit k.
proof_idea: First digits correspond to fractional parts of m log_10 2 lying in [log_10 k, log_10(k+1)).
exam_use: A memorable application of equidistribution to leading-digit asymptotics.
pitfalls: Benford frequencies describe a limiting distribution, not exact finite-sample frequencies.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Lp convergence upgrade
summary: If X is in Lp for p>1, the Birkhoff averages converge in Lp as well as almost surely.
proof_idea: Use maximal or domination arguments for the averages and uniform integrability of powers.
exam_use: Useful when a problem asks for convergence in norm rather than only almost surely.
pitfalls: Do not infer Lp convergence from L1 convergence without an Lp bound or uniform integrability.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Range growth for stationary increments
summary: For a stationary-increment walk S_n, the range R_n=|{S_1,...,S_n}| satisfies R_n/n -> E(1_A|I), where A is the event of never returning to 0 after time 0.
proof_idea: Bound R_n below by future-noncollision indicators and above by finite-window noncollision indicators, then apply Birkhoff and monotone convergence.
exam_use: Connects recurrence questions to an ergodic average of escape events.
pitfalls: The limit is conditional on I; only in ergodic cases does it reduce to P(A).

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Zero conditional drift implies recurrence on Z
summary: For stationary integer-valued increments with E|X_1|<infinity and E(X_1|I)=0, the walk returns to 0 infinitely often almost surely.
proof_idea: Birkhoff gives S_n/n -> 0, so the range is sublinear; the range theorem forces escape probability zero, and stationarity then gives infinitely many returns.
exam_use: Generalizes the Chung-Fuchs recurrence intuition beyond iid increments.
pitfalls: The condition is E(X_1|I)=0, not merely EX_1=0; mixtures of positive and negative drifts are counterexamples.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Kac return-time theorem
summary: For a stationary process that hits A almost surely, the successive return gaps to A are stationary under P(.|X_0 in A), and E(T_1|X_0 in A)=1/P(X_0 in A).
proof_idea: Embed the process two-sided, decompose by the last visit before time 0, and sum tail probabilities of the first return.
exam_use: Turns visits to a set into regenerative cycles and recovers mean return time 1/pi(x) for Markov chains.
pitfalls: Conditioning on X_0 in A is essential; the first waiting time from an arbitrary stationary time has a length-biased flavor.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Cycle trick for stationary measures
summary: Expected occupation of B during a return cycle to A equals P(X_0 in B)/P(X_0 in A) when A and B are disjoint and A is recurrent.
proof_idea: Sum indicators over the cycle and use stationarity to convert cycle occupation counts into one-time probabilities.
exam_use: Useful for constructing stationary measures of Markov chains from excursions.
pitfalls: The denominator is the stationary mass of A; the formula is not a conditional probability of hitting B before returning.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Stationary renewal waiting-time bias
summary: For a stationary {0,1} process conditioned on X_0=1, the first return law satisfies Pbar(T_1=n)=Pbar(T_1>=n)/Ebar T_1 in the iid-gap renewal case.
proof_idea: Count which positions inside a renewal interval can serve as the stationary origin.
exam_use: Explains inspection-paradox corrections in stationary renewal processes.
pitfalls: The distribution seen from a stationary time differs from the raw interarrival distribution.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Subadditive ergodic theorem
summary: For a stationary subadditive array X_{m,n} with integrability and a linear lower expectation bound, X_{0,n}/n converges almost surely and in L1 to a limit with mean inf_m E X_{0,m}/m.
proof_idea: Use Fekete's lemma for expectations, Birkhoff on block increments for the limsup, then a covering argument for the liminf.
exam_use: The main tool for limits of optimal costs, passage times, longest subsequences, and products.
pitfalls: Check the exact stationarity assumptions; Liggett's version is weaker than Kingman's original in useful applications.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Fekete step for expected subadditive arrays
summary: If a_n=E X_{0,n} and a_m+a_{n-m}>=a_n, then a_n/n converges to inf_m a_m/m.
proof_idea: Write n=km+r, iterate subadditivity, divide by n, and let n grow with fixed m.
exam_use: Use whenever the deterministic expectation part of a subadditive theorem appears.
pitfalls: The inequality direction is easy to mix up because the array convention is X_{0,m}+X_{m,n}>=X_{0,n}.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Birkhoff as the additive special case
summary: If X_{m,n}=xi_{m+1}+...+xi_n for a stationary integrable sequence, then the subadditive theorem reduces to Birkhoff.
proof_idea: The subadditive inequality becomes equality, and block stationarity is inherited from the xi sequence.
exam_use: A quick consistency check for the hypotheses of the subadditive theorem.
pitfalls: Do not lose the indexing: X_{m,n} covers increments after m through n.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Range as a subadditive example
summary: The number of distinct sites visited between times m+1 and n is subadditive, so the normalized range has an almost sure and L1 limit.
proof_idea: The union of sites in two adjacent time intervals covers the sites in the whole interval, giving X_{0,m}+X_{m,n}>=X_{0,n}.
exam_use: Provides a second route to existence of range-growth rates even when the rate is not identified.
pitfalls: The theorem gives existence of the limit, not the recurrence classification by itself.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Longest common subsequence limit
summary: For two ergodic stationary sequences, the longest common subsequence length L_{0,n} satisfies L_{0,n}/n -> gamma, where gamma=sup_m E L_{0,m}/m.
proof_idea: Use superadditivity of L over adjacent blocks, or subadditivity of -L, and apply the subadditive ergodic theorem.
exam_use: Recognize optimization lengths as candidates for subadditive limits.
pitfalls: The theorem proves existence of gamma but usually not its numerical value.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Products of positive random matrices
summary: For stationary positive matrices with integrable log entries, n^{-1} log of product entries converges almost surely to a common growth rate.
proof_idea: Take minus logs of positive product entries to create a subadditive array; compare entries and norms to transfer the limit.
exam_use: A probability route to Lyapunov-exponent-type limits.
pitfalls: Strict positivity and log-integrability matter; zeros or heavy tails can break the logarithmic comparison.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Increasing subsequences in random permutations
summary: Poissonizing random permutations and applying subadditivity shows the longest increasing subsequence has an n^{1/2} order limit constant.
proof_idea: Represent permutations by Poisson points in squares, use superadditivity of increasing paths, then de-Poissonize with the Poisson count law.
exam_use: A classic example of turning a combinatorial optimization problem into an ergodic limit.
pitfalls: The subadditive argument gives the existence of the constant; identifying it as 2 requires deeper work.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Poisson square time change
summary: If tau(n) is the side length of the square containing the first n points of a rate-one planar Poisson process, then tau(n)/sqrt(n) -> 1 almost surely.
proof_idea: The number of points in the square of side sqrt(n) is a sum of independent mean-one Poisson increments, so the strong law controls the inverse time.
exam_use: Used to pass from Poissonized increasing paths back to fixed-size random permutations.
pitfalls: The scale is sqrt(n) because the square area is t^2.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: First-passage percolation time constant
summary: For iid nonnegative edge passage times on Z^d with suitable integrability, t(0,nu)/n converges almost surely to a deterministic time constant.
proof_idea: Passage times satisfy t(0,nu)<=t(0,mu)+t(mu,nu), so X_{m,n}=t(mu,nu) is subadditive; tail triviality makes the limit constant.
exam_use: Core template for metric growth models and random media.
pitfalls: Finite mean of one edge is sufficient in the simple statement but can be weakened; without integrability the limit may be infinite.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: First-passage percolation integrability condition
summary: A weaker useful condition is that the minimum of 2d independent edge times has finite mean, equivalently integral_0^infty (1-F(x))^{2d} dx < infinity.
proof_idea: Use disjoint neighboring paths to dominate one-step passage times by minima of several edge variables.
exam_use: Helps check when a finite time constant exists for heavy-tailed edge weights.
pitfalls: This condition concerns the minimum of several edges, not the edge variable itself.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Age-dependent branching process speed
summary: In an age-dependent branching process with no extinction at birth and finite lifetimes, the first birth time in generation n divided by n converges almost surely.
proof_idea: Define X_{m,n} as the additional time from the first generation-m individual to a generation-n descendant and apply Liggett's subadditive theorem.
exam_use: Shows why the weaker Liggett hypotheses are useful beyond Kingman's original stationary-array assumptions.
pitfalls: The stronger all-triples subadditivity can fail because the fastest later descendant need not descend from the fastest earlier individual.

source: Rick Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, extracted Chapter 6 PDF
title: Branching speed via lifetime large deviations
summary: For mean offspring mu and lifetime sums with rate c(a), the asymptotic earliest-generation speed is gamma=inf{a: log mu - c(a)>0}.
proof_idea: Compare expected counts of generation-n individuals born by time an with branching-process survival when the count grows supercritically.
exam_use: Connects subadditive limits with large-deviation rate functions.
pitfalls: The subadditive theorem gives existence; this formula requires additional branching and large-deviation analysis.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Invariant sigma-field and invariant random variables
summary: Show invariant events form a sigma-field by pullback closure; prove an I-measurable random variable is invariant by checking rational cuts, and conversely use invariant inverse images.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.1; identifying E(X|I) and invariant limits.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Strict representative for almost invariant sets
summary: From an almost invariant set, form a forward pullback union B and then C=intersection phi^{-n}B; C is strictly invariant and differs only by a null set.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.2; replacing mod-null invariance by exact invariance.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Direct density proof of irrational rotation ergodicity
summary: Use pigeonhole gaps to prove the irrational orbit is dense, approximate positive-measure sets by high-density intervals, and contradict coexistence of invariant A and A^c with positive measure.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.3; proving ergodicity without Fourier series.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Two-sided extension of a stationary sequence
summary: Define finite-dimensional laws on integer-indexed coordinates by shifting all indices into the nonnegative side, then use stationarity for consistency and Kolmogorov extension.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.4; return-time and cycle arguments needing negative times.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Stationarity and ergodicity pass to factors
summary: For Y_k=g(X_k,X_{k+1},...), stationarity follows from shifted future laws; invariant events for Y pull back to invariant events for X, so ergodicity passes to Y.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.5; creating stationary derived processes.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Random phase makes iid blocks stationary and ergodic
summary: Concatenate iid blocks and start at an independent uniform phase; one-step shifts rotate the phase and occasionally shift the iid block sequence, giving stationarity and ergodicity.
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.6; block constructions with stationary output.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples
title: Gauss map invariant density by branch telescoping
summary: For phi(x)=1/x-floor(1/x), split into branches x=1/(k+y); the transformed density sum telescopes to 1/(1+y).
proof_idea: Convert stationary processes to shift or measure-preserving models; prove invariance through pullbacks; use density, factor maps, random phases, and branch decompositions for examples.
exam_use: Exercise 6.1.7; verifying the continued-fraction invariant measure.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities
title: Lp upgrade of Birkhoff by truncation
summary: Prove Lp convergence for bounded observables by bounded convergence, then truncate X and control the tail using Jensen plus Lp contraction of conditional expectation.
proof_idea: Upgrade Birkhoff convergence by truncation and Jensen; compare moving functions to a fixed limit using tail suprema or Cesaro L1 bounds; derive maximal inequalities by centering with a threshold.
exam_use: Exercise 6.2.1; upgrading ergodic averages from L1 to Lp.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities
title: Moving observable ergodic average
summary: Decompose averages of g_m(phi^m) into the fixed g average plus errors; control a.s. errors by tail suprema and L1 errors by Cesaro means.
proof_idea: Upgrade Birkhoff convergence by truncation and Jensen; compare moving functions to a fixed limit using tail suprema or Cesaro L1 bounds; derive maximal inequalities by centering with a threshold.
exam_use: Exercise 6.2.2; triangular or time-varying observables in ergodic averages.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities
title: Wiener maximal inequality from maximal ergodic lemma
summary: Apply the maximal ergodic lemma to Y=X-alpha so the positivity event of the shifted partial sums is exactly {max S_j/j > alpha}.
proof_idea: Upgrade Birkhoff convergence by truncation and Jensen; compare moving functions to a fixed limit using tail suprema or Cesaro L1 bounds; derive maximal inequalities by centering with a threshold.
exam_use: Exercise 6.2.3; deriving maximal probability bounds for ergodic averages.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias
title: Range expectation by last-visit decomposition
summary: Count each visited site by its last visit time; stationarity makes the probability of no future revisit over n-m steps equal to g_{n-m}.
proof_idea: Count range by last visits, identify escape probability through range growth, and use two-sided stationarity to turn return cycles into occupation and waiting-time identities.
exam_use: Exercise 6.3.1; expected range identities for stationary-increment walks.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias
title: Positive drift escape probability from range growth
summary: With ergodic integer increments bounded above by one and positive mean, the range grows at the same rate as the running maximum; Theorem 6.3.1 identifies the rate with P(escape).
proof_idea: Count range by last visits, identify escape probability through range growth, and use two-sided stationarity to turn return cycles into occupation and waiting-time identities.
exam_use: Exercise 6.3.2; recurrence/escape probabilities under stationary increments.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias
title: Cycle trick occupation ratio
summary: Use a two-sided stationary version and partition by the last visit to A before time 0; expected B-occupation in an A-cycle equals P(X0 in B)/P(X0 in A).
proof_idea: Count range by last visits, identify escape probability through range growth, and use two-sided stationarity to turn return cycles into occupation and waiting-time identities.
exam_use: Exercise 6.3.3; constructing stationary measures from excursions.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias
title: Stationary renewal first-waiting bias
summary: Under conditioning on a renewal at time 0, stationarity of return gaps gives P(T=n)=Pbar(T>=n)/Ebar T for the waiting time seen from a stationary time.
proof_idea: Count range by last visits, identify escape probability through range growth, and use two-sided stationarity to turn return cycles into occupation and waiting-time identities.
exam_use: Exercise 6.3.4; inspection-paradox and stationary renewal calculations.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching
title: Arbitrarily slow deterministic subadditive convergence
summary: Set X_{m,m+k}=f(k) with f(k)/k decreasing; subadditivity follows by comparing both pieces to f(n)/n, so the expectation convergence can be as slow as f(n)/n.
proof_idea: Check deterministic subadditivity rates, use first-moment bounds for subsequence problems, exploit Poisson greedy paths, and build exponential martingales for branching-process speed bounds.
exam_use: Exercise 6.5.1; understanding limits of Fekete-type convergence rates.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching
title: Longest common subsequence first-moment bounds
summary: Compute small-n expectations for lower bounds and bound P(L_n >= K) by the expected number of matching index-pair subsequences, binom(n,K)^2 2^{-K}.
proof_idea: Check deterministic subadditivity rates, use first-moment bounds for subsequence problems, exploit Poisson greedy paths, and build exponential martingales for branching-process speed bounds.
exam_use: Exercise 6.5.2; bounding LCS constants.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching
title: Poisson greedy path lower bound
summary: Choose successive Poisson points minimizing x+y in the northeast quadrant; the increment sum has tail exp(-t^2/2), giving mean coordinate step sqrt(pi/8) and gamma >= sqrt(8/pi).
proof_idea: Check deterministic subadditivity rates, use first-moment bounds for subsequence problems, exploit Poisson greedy paths, and build exponential martingales for branching-process speed bounds.
exam_use: Exercise 6.5.3; lower bounds for Hammersley's increasing path constant.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

source: Solved Durrett Chapter 6 exercises and exercise viki records
section: 6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching
title: Permutation LIS upper bound by first moment
summary: For a fixed k-subset of indices, the values are increasing with probability 1/k!, so E J_k^n=binom(n,k)/k!; Stirling with k~alpha sqrt(n) gives gamma <= e.
proof_idea: Check deterministic subadditivity rates, use first-moment bounds for subsequence problems, exploit Poisson greedy paths, and build exponential martingales for branching-process speed bounds.

section: 6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching
title: Branching Laplace martingale speed bound
summary: Normalize the generation-n Laplace sum by (mu phi(theta))^n to get a nonnegative martingale; Markov's inequality bounds early birth events when exp(-theta a)/(mu phi(theta))>1.
proof_idea: Check deterministic subadditivity rates, use first-moment bounds for subsequence problems, exploit Poisson greedy paths, and build exponential martingales for branching-process speed bounds.
exam_use: Exercise 6.5.5; age-dependent branching speed upper-tail bounds.
pitfalls: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.
